coredata.dc:title: Rational construct CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance for degradation of sulfathiazole
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zhongzheng
Xie, Xiaoyun
Li, Shan
Song, Mengxi
Liang, Guiwei
Zhao, Jing
Wang, Zhaowei
coredata.dc:description: The novel heterojunction photocatalysts also known as carbon quantum dots-decorated BiOCOOH/ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN) were designed, where carbon quantum dots (CQDs) acted as mediators to shuttle electrons between BiOCOOH and ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (uCN). The physicochemical properties of as-obtained composites were systematically characterized by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffractometry, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN consisting of 50 wt% ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (uCN) and 4 mL CQDs solution manifested the optimal photoactivity. After 90 min of LED lamp irradiation, the STZ degradation efficiency by 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN reached 99.28% ± 1.04, and the corresponding degree of mineralization was 49.58% ± 1.36. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was owing to the fast photogenerated charges transfer and separation revealed by photoluminescence (PL), transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements. By means of the radicals quenching experiments and electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) analysis, it was demonstrated that •O2 – and •OH were the dominant reactive species of STZ degradation. Besides, the effects of catalyst dosage (0.2–1.2 g/L), pH value (3.0–11.0), natural organic matter and different anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −) on the photocatalytic activity of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite were systematically explored. 17 main intermediates were found based on high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) detection. Eventually, the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite showed favorable reusability in recycling experiments, and displayed satisfactory degradation abilities for STZ in deionized water (99.28% ± 1.04), tap water (97.15% ± 1.16), river water (96.44% ± 0.76), and wastewater treatment plant effluent (96.19% ± 1.20).

head.ce:title: Rational construct CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance for degradation of sulfathiazole

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel heterojunction photocatalysts also known as carbon quantum dots-decorated BiOCOOH/ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN) were designed, where carbon quantum dots (CQDs) acted as mediators to shuttle electrons between BiOCOOH and ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (uCN). The physicochemical properties of as-obtained composites were systematically characterized by scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffractometry, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN consisting of 50 wt% ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (uCN) and 4 mL CQDs solution manifested the optimal photoactivity. After 90 min of LED lamp irradiation, the STZ degradation efficiency by 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN reached 99.28% ± 1.04, and the corresponding degree of mineralization was 49.58% ± 1.36. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was owing to the fast photogenerated charges transfer and separation revealed by photoluminescence (PL), transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements. By means of the radicals quenching experiments and electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) analysis, it was demonstrated that •O2 – and •OH were the dominant reactive species of STZ degradation. Besides, the effects of catalyst dosage (0.2–1.2 g/L), pH value (3.0–11.0), natural organic matter and different anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −) on the photocatalytic activity of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite were systematically explored. 17 main intermediates were found based on high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) detection. Eventually, the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite showed favorable reusability in recycling experiments, and displayed satisfactory degradation abilities for STZ in deionized water (99.28% ± 1.04), tap water (97.15% ± 1.16), river water (96.44% ± 0.76), and wastewater treatment plant effluent (96.19% ± 1.20).

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides, a cut-price class of antibiotics, are frequently detected in aquatic environment across the world because of their widespread use and unlimited discharge [1]. Among the sulfonamides, sulfathiazole (STZ) is widely used as veterinary medicine [2]. According to the previous research, STZ is found in surface water and sewage effluent at a concentration of ng/L and ug/L respectively but in agricultural wastewater with a concentration of mg/L [3–5]. Due to its potential toxicity and risk of developing antibiotic resistance genes, STZ will threaten aquatic ecosystem and human health even with lower concentration [6]. However, the researchers revealed no significant degradation of STZ after 3 weeks of hydrolysis reaction [7]. Hence, it is urgent to seek the energy conservation and efficient treatment techniques to eliminate STZ and other pollutants. As known, ozonation [8], membrane process [9], adsorption process [10], biological process [11], electrochemical technology [12], photocatalytic degradation [13] and etc. are powerful methods for wastewater treatment. In comparison with other methods, photocatalysis emerges is considered to be a powerful, easy-operation and cost-effective to remove various contaminants, such as pesticides [14], antibiotics [13], dyes [15], hormones [16].

Over the past years, BiOCOOH has attracted great attention due to its peculiar layered structure and low toxicity [17,18]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic activity of BiOCOOH is hindered by the wide band gap (3.4 eV), poor light absorption (<370 nm) and slow rate of photoinduced charge transfer [19]. To the best of our knowledge, fabrication of heterojunction system is an effective way for improving the photocatalytic activity of one-component catalyst, since it can enlarge the light absorption range and decrease the efficient of charge recombination. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a visible-light-driven material with appropriate band gap (2.7 eV), thermal and chemical stability, which has been widely used to construct a heterojunction structure for enhancing the photocatalytic activity [20,21]. Therefore, Cui and co-workers have constructed the BiOCOOH/bulk g-C3N4 hybrid to improve photocatalytic performance [22]. Although the coupling of BiOCOOH and g-C3N4 enhanced visible light absorption ability and photogenerated carriers transfer, its photocatalytic activity was not satisfactory [19]. According to reports, BiOCOOH/g-C3N4 is a traditional type-II heterojunction photocatalyst where the redox ability of photoelectron and hole are weaker [19,21]. To overcome this problem, it is necessary to modify BiOCOOH/g-C3N4 with suitable nanomaterials.

Fortunately, the dual semiconductor systems consisting of solid-state electron mediators has been developed recently, which possesses superb redox ability along with high photoinduced electron-hole pair separation efficiency [23,24]. In systems, the solid-state electron mediators such as Au, Ag, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can serve as mediators to shuttle the electrons between two semiconductors [25,26]. Compared with noble metals, CQDs with a size below 10 nm are more suitable as charge transmission bridges to construct dual semiconductor photocatalytic system with mediators due to its impressive electron transfer, electron reservoir and low-cost properties [27]. In addition, the upconversion photoluminescence effects of CQDs will strengthen visible light absorption capacity of catalyst [28]. As a result, CQDs is a promising solid-state electron mediator that can be added in BiOCOOH/g-C3N4 complex to modulate the migration route of photogenerated carriers.

The chief purposes of this research were (i) synthesis of novel CQDs decorated BiOCOOH/ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN) heterojunction photocatalyst for efficient decomposition of STZ; (ii) evaluation the impacts of different conditions (i.e., catalyst dosage, solution pH, natural organic matter, inorganic anions and real water matrices) on the photodegradation process; (iii) investigation the stability and reusability of CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN by recycling runs; (iv) discussion the probable photocatalytic mechanism and STZ degradation pathways; (v) comparison and evaluation the energy consumption of degradation of STZ by CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite under xenon lamp and LED lamp.

Melamine (99.0%) was obtained from Kexin chemical Co., Shanghai. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O ≥ 99.0%) and nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) were purchased from Zhongqin Chemical Co., China. Citrate acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O ≥ 99.5%) was bought from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co. Ltd. Ethylenediamine (Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd.), sodium formate (HCOONa, Caixin chemical Co. Ltd), terephthalic acid (TA, Guangfu fine chemical research institute, Tianjin) were all of analytical grade. Acetonitrile and trifluoroacetic acid were HPLC grade. Sulfathiazole (STZ, > 98.0%) was provided by Tokyo chemical industry Co., Ltd. Tap water was taken from the Lanzhou water group (China). River water was collected from the Yellow River and wastewater treatment plant effluent was taken from Yanerwan wastewater treatment plant in Lanzhou, Gansu province.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of uCN nanosheets and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (uCN) were synthesized by direct thermal oxidation “etching” process of bulk g-C3N4 at air atmosphere [26]. Melamine was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 5.5 °C min−1 to obtain the yellow bulk g-C3N4 and milled it into powders. Subsequently, the bulk g-C3N4 powders were introduced into an alumina crucible without a cover and thermally exfoliated into uCN by calcination at 500 °C for 2 h.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from citrate acid and ethylenediamine via a hydrothermal method [27]. Specifically, 1.0507 g C6H8O7·H2O and 335 μL ethylenediamine were added in 10 mL water. The mixture solution was then moved into Teflon-lined autoclave (KH100, Taikang technology Co., Ltd.) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixed solution was dialyzed for 24 h to obtain the CQDs solution and stored at – 4 °C.

In a typical synthesis, 0.27 g uCN and 0.068 g HCOONa were ultrasonically dispersed in 30 mL distilled water. Meanwhile, 20 mL glycol solution containing Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.486 g) was added into the above suspension. After stirring for 30 min by magnetic stirrer (RH digital white, IKA, Germany), CQDs aqueous dispersion (2 mL, 4 mL, 6 mL or 8 mL) was further dripped into the reaction suspension. The mixed suspension was stirred for another 30 min and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave (150 °C, 10 h). The products were centrifuged for 5 min by centrifuge (L550, cence, Hunan) at 4000 rotations per minute, and rinsed with ethanol and deionized water. Thereafter the obtained precipitates were dried for 12 h at 60 °C and labeled as x-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN, where x represented the volume of CQDs stock solution.

In order to optimize the composition of uCN in catalyst, y BiOCOOH/uCN (y = 5%, 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%, 60%) were synthesized using the same method without CQDs, where y was corresponded to the mass ratio of uCN and BiOCOOH.

The morphology and elementary composition of samples were identified by scan electron microscope (SEM, JSM-5600LV) equipped with the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Oxford instruments IE250), field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6701F), transmission electron microscopy and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM, FEI TECNAI G2, USA). The crystal structures and infrared absorption spectra of photocatalysts were collected using an X-ray diffractometry (XRD, D/max-2400, Rigaku) and a Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd), respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer (Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were investigated by a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) under an excitation wavelength at 270 nm. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were analyzed on a Cary 500 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). The total organic was determined on a TOC analyzer (elementar).

Photodegradation activities of all expected samples were assessed by decomposing STZ in a homemade LED lamp photo-reactor setup. The relative intensity of LED light source (50 W) and the schematic of photocatalytic chamber (L × W × H = 55 × 48 × 65 cm) were shown in Fig. S1. Clearly, the emitting spectrum of LED lamp was 380 – 780 nm, whose dominant wavelength and optical efficiency were 475 nm and 68.91 m/W. In each round of the experiment, the initial pH of STZ solution was corrected to the 6.60 ± 0.05. Prior to irradiation, 80 mg sample was added to 100 mL of STZ solution (10 mg/L, pHinitial = 6.60) and stirred for 30 min in dark to reach an adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then the suspension was irradiated by LED lamp to start the photocatalytic reaction. At predetermined time intervals, aliquots were collected and filtered through 0.22 μm nylon membranes for quantitative analysis. Additionally, to evaluate the application potential of as-synthesized hybrid in water treatment, the impacts of diverse parameters (i.e. catalyst dosage, pH, natural organic matter, inorganic species and actual water body) were conducted. The experiments were performed in triplicate.

The concentration of STZ was analyzed by UHPLC (Ultimate 3000, Dionex, USA) with a RS diode array detector and Accucore XL C18 column. The mobile phase was acetonitrile-0.1‰ trifluoroacetic acid (20:80, v/v) with a flow rate of 0.7 mL/min. The detection wavelength was 284 nm and the injection volume was 20 μL. The STZ degradation byproducts were detected on HRMS (Orbitrap Elite, Velos Pro, Thermo Scientific, USA). An electrospray interface (ESI) source in positive ionization mode was used and a full scan acquisition between m/z 100 – 500 was performed to conduct mass spectroscopy. Fluorescence and TOC changes of STZ solution during photolysis processes were measured by Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) and Vario TOC analyzer (Elementar Vario TOC select), respectively.

Fig. 2 demonstrated TEM and HRTEM micrographs of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the uCN was typical graphite-shaped carbon nitride architectures (translucent, irregular, thin nanosheet) and co-existed with BiOCOOH nanosheets in the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite. The CQDs were embedded in the 50% BiOCOOH/uCN matrix, whose diameter distribution was 2 – 10 nm, which was in accord with SEM analysis (Fig. 2(b)). The SAED pattern depicted a bright concentric diffraction ring, revealing poly-crystalline nature of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN (inset in Fig. 2(b)) [29]. And the lattice stripes of 0.315, 0.272, 0.22 and 0.326 nm were observed from HRTEM (Fig. 2(c-g)), corresponding to the (102) and (110) planes of BiOCOOH, (101) spacing of graphitic carbon and (002) plane of uCN, respectively [18,21,30]. The results unambiguously revealed that the hetero-junctions among CQDs, BiOCOOH and uCN were developed during the hydrothermal process.

Fig. 3 (a) showed XRD patterns of BiOCOOH, 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst. For uCN, the diffraction peaks at 27.4° and 13.1° were assigned to (002) and (100) planes, corresponding to interlayer-stacking reflection of the C–N melon segments and in-plane repeated units, respectively [31]. For pure BiOCOOH, the crystal structure could be indexed to the tetragonal BiOCOOH (JCPDS No. 35–0939). With respect to CQDs, a broad peak centered at 25° might be matched with the highly disordered carbon atoms (Fig. S3), which was well consistent with previous reports [32]. The 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN nanohybrids showed the similar XRD pattern of pristine BiOCOOH, which was ascribed to the poor crystallinity of uCN and the high dispersion of CQDs [26,33]. Besides, the (102) facet of 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN obviously became stronger and sharper compared to the unmixed BiOCOOH, implying the formation of an intimate contact between BiOCOOH and uCN.

Fig. 3(b) exhibited FT-IR spectra of as-fabricated catalysts. In the uCN spectrum, the absorption signals at 808 cm−1, 1000 – 1700 cm−1 were corresponded to the skeletal vibrations of the triazine units and C–N heterocycles [34]. The band at 3000 – 3400 cm−1 was related to breathing mode of N–H (uncondensed amine groups) or C–OH (absorbed water species) [35]. Except for the new Bi–O (557 cm−1) peak, the FT-IR spectra of 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN were both similar to that of uCN, revealing that BiOCOOH had been coupled with uCN. However, the absorption peak at 3000 – 3400 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN became weaker compared to 50% BiOCOOH/uCN composite, which might be attributed to the formation of amide bonds between CQDs and 50% BiOCOOH/uCN [36]. FT-IR spectra further confirmed the successful combination of CQDs, BiOCOOH and uCN.

Fig. 4 manifested XPS spectra of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN, which was used to analyze its surface elements and valence states. As could be seen from the full-scan spectra (Fig. 4(a)), C, N, Bi and O elements were existed in the surface of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum elucidated three peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.2 eV and 287.7 eV, representing to the C–C bond, C–NH2, NC–C species respectively (Fig. 4(b)) [26]. The peaks at 398.1 eV and 399.1 eV of N 1s XPS spectrum were ascribed to CN–C and N–C3 bonding. Another peak at 400.7 eV was associated with amino groups (NHx) and amide bonds (–NH–CO–) that formed between –NH2 and –COOH (Fig. 4(c)) [37]. In the Bi 4f XPS spectrum, the typical symmetric peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 located at 158.7 eV and 164.0 eV were indexed to Bi3+ of BiOCOOH (Fig. 4(d)) [22]. In the O spectrum (Fig. 4(e)), the O 1s was deconvoluted to Bi–O bond (529.5 eV), –OH/–COOH group (531.3 eV) and the absorbed H2O (532.6 eV) [38]. Combined with HRTEM and FTIR results, it could be deduced that the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN nanohybrid had been fabricated through thermal polymerization and subsequent hydrothermal method.

The optical absorption property and charge separation capacity of photocatalyst could be indicated by UV–vis DRS and PL spectra, which were closely related to photocatalytic performance of material. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (a), the band gap absorption edges of pure BiOCOOH and uCN were about 367 nm and 468 nm, suggesting that the visible light would be utilized by uCN and pristine BiOCOOH was merely driven by UV light. Interestingly, the light absorption region of 50% BiOCOOH/uCN complex was visibly enlarged, which might be due to the formation of hetero-junction structure between BiOCOOH and uCN. In further, the absorption intensity in 350 – 750 nm was further enhanced after introducing CQDs to 50% BiOCOOH/uCN complex, which was favor to produce more electron-hole pairs and give rise to higher photodegradation activity. At the same time, the steep absorption edge of 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN meant that visible-light absorption was not due to the jump of impurity level, but to energy band transition [32]. The band gap energy (Eg) of semiconductor was evaluated according to the Tauc formula, which was exhibited in Fig. S4 [22]. Clearly, the Eg of BiOCOOH and uCN were 3.48 eV and 2.70 eV, in keeping with previous studies [39]. As for PL spectra, Fig. 5(b) showed the emission peak of uCN centered at ∼ 460 nm, which might largely stem from the π → π electronic transitions in aromatic conjugation framework [34]. Notably, the fluorescence emission peaks of photocatalysts followed an order of BiOCOOH < 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN < 50% BiOCOOH/uCN < uCN, revealing that the recombination of photo-induced carriers was restrained in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN nanocomposites. The separation of photo-generated carriers could be further proved by the electrochemical properties of as-prepared samples. Fig. 5(c) exhibited the photocurrent spectra for photocatalysts. As expected, the highest photocurrent intensity was found for the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite. The greater 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocurrent exhibited that the photo-generated charges could be separated more effectively, which was consistent with its photocatalytic activities. Fig. 5(d) was electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) plots of samples. It was clear that the capacitive arc radius of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was lower than that of BiOCOOH, uCN and 50% BiOCOOH/uCN illustrating that the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN heterojunction formation accelerated the carriers transport and decreased the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes.

Photocatalytic activities of as-synthesized catalysts for STZ degradation were tested under LED lamp irradiation. As seen from Fig. 6 (a), no appreciable photolysis of STZ was observed in the presence of light or pure BiOCOOH. Nevertheless, the photodegradation performance of BiOCOOH/uCN nanocomposites were remarkably enhanced, which was probably due to the formation of heterojunction. With increasing uCN content (5% – 50%), the apparent reaction rate constant (Kapp) of STZ degradation increased to a maximum value of around 0.0348 min−1 (50% BiOCOOH/uCN), after which it decreased to nearly 0.0258 min−1 (Fig. 6(c)). It was because excessive uCN would agglomerate and overlap on the surface of BiOCOOH, reducing surface contact and inhibiting the formation of heterojunction [40]. Therefore, 50% BiOCOOH/uCN was chosen to optimize the content of CQDs in ternary nanocomposite. Fig. 6(b) displayed the effect of CQDs on photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composites. And results portrayed that the photocatalytic activities were improved when 50% BiOCOOH/uCN incorporated with CQDs. Concretely, the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN nanohybrid showed the highest photocatalytic activity with a corresponding Kapp of 0.0528 min−1 (Fig. 6(d)), which was about 4 times higher than pure uCN. However, with the further increase of CQDs, the photocatalytic activity would decrease. This could be explained by the fact that CQDs at low level facilitated the charge separation, while excessive CQDs impeded the light absorption of semiconductor [32]. Moreover, the adsorption experiments were performed to disclose the adsorption capacity of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN catalyst. Fig. S5 showed that the adsorption of STZ (0.2 mg/g) by 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was inferior, which indirectly indicated that the removal of STZ relied on photodegradation rather than the adsorption reaction. The UHPLC spectra of STZ degradation over 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN under LED lamp irradiation was shown in Fig. S6. The peak of STZ was about at 4.2 min and gradually decreased. With the increase of time, a new peak appeared at 3.4 min, suggesting that new intermediates were produced during reaction process.

Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (3D EEMs) have already been used to roughly detect the molecular changes of organic pollutants in photolysis process [41,42]. As described in Fig. 7 (a), no fluorescence appeared in the STZ original solution. After the 20 min photolysis reaction, a blurry peak at λex/λem = 235 – 247/250 – 400 nm was monitored, which could be labeled as fulvic-like compounds (Fig. 7(b)) [43]. With lengthening illumination time in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system, the peak signal intensity was continually strengthened (Fig. 7(c, d)). This elucidated that STZ molecules mineralized or decomposed into other intermediates during the photolysis process. Subsequently, the mineralization degree of STZ was further quantified by the total organic carbon (TOC). As shown in Fig. S7, after 90 min and 120 min reactions, the removal efficiencies of TOC were 49.58% and 62.16% respectively. In addition, the removal efficiency of TOC remained monotone increasing with illumination time. Hence, it was reasonable to deduce that STZ could be totally mineralized by extending the reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN dosage and solution pH
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the kinetic behaviors of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system on STZ photodegradation would be susceptible to reaction system parameters. First of all, the influence of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN dosage (0.2 – 1.6 g/L) was investigated, and the result was presented in Fig. 8 (a, b). It was apparent in Fig. 8(a) that the removal rate of STZ gradually strengthened to the maximum value of 99.28% ± 1.04 as photocatalyst dosage increased from 0.2 to 0.8 g/L. Nevertheless, when the catalyst dosage exceeded 0.8 g/L, the removal efficiency tended to decline. Similar phenomenon was reflected between the Kapp and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN dosage (Fig. 8(b)). This was because the active site was limiting factor at low 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN dosage. The number of active site improved with increasing catalyst dosage (0.2 – 0.8 g/L), which accelerated the STZ decomposition [44]. Whereas, the Kapp decline was caused by the light shielding effect of excess nano-particles (>0.8 g/L) [45].

What's more, the solution pH would influence the status of contaminant and photocatalyst, as a result to affecting the photocatalytic degradation of STZ. As exhibited in Fig. S8, the point of zero charge (pHpzc) of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was ca. 7.34. The pKa of STZ was 2.40 and 7.10, thus the pH-dependent states of STZ could be described according the Eqs. (1) [46].
(1)

Fig. 8(c) showed the effect of initial pH on the photocatalytic degradation of STZ in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system. Apparently, the photodegradation efficiencies of STZ at pH 3.00 – 9.00 all could reach up to 98%, which indicated that 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN had superior photocatalytic activity at acidic or week alkaline circumstance. Yet, the removal efficiency of STZ at pH = 11 was dropped to 89%, possibly owing to the electrostatic repulsion. Additionally, the solution pH after reaction was also monitored. With the decomposition of STZ, pH was reduced from the initial 3.00, 4.99, 7.03, 9.00, 11.00 to 3.04, 4.13, 4.55, 5.23 and 8.07 respectively, which suggested that acidic intermediates were generated during the photolysis process. Unlike the tendency of removal rate, the Kapp decreased from 0.0492 (pH = 3.00) to 0.0458 min−1 (pH = 4.99), then increased when pH was varied from 4.99 to 7.03, and further shrank dramatically at pH 7.03 – 11.00 (Fig. 8(d)). The explanation was that the H+ could consume •O2 – radical and the concentration of H+ would decrease with pH increase. Thus, there existed more •O2 – to degrade STZ when pH increased from 4.99 to 7.03, leading to the stepwise enhanced Kapp [13]. Subsequently, an electrostatic repulsion might occur between deprotonized STZ and the negatively charged 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN in the pH range of 7.34 – 11.00, which gave rise to the downtrend of Kapp. Nevertheless, the exceptional Kapp at extreme acidic condition (pH = 3.00) was likely ascribed to the smaller particle size of the aggregations of nanocomposite at lower pH [47]. The minor agglomerates of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN would possess higher utilization rate of visible light and produce more occasion to contact with STZ pollutant.

Natural organic matter (HA as reference) was the prevalent constituent in actual water matrix, which was scavenger for active species. The effect of HA upon 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis reaction system has been researched and the result was shown in Fig. 9 (a). Obviously, the Kapp of STZ after addition HA followed the sequence of 0.0528 min−1 (0 mg/L HA) > 0.0386 min−1 (5 mg/L HA) and 0.0391 min−1 (15 mg/L HA) > 0.0311 min−1 (20 mg/L HA) > 0.0270 min−1 (30 mg/L HA). Hence, it was obtained that HA significantly inhibited the decomposition of STZ in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system. This “NOM effect” was explained by two reasons: (i) HA enshrouded or displaced ligands sited on the surface of the photocatalyst, impeding light absorption and decreasing its active sites; (ii) the introduction of HA in the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system consumed active radicals, leading to the reduced photodegradation of STZ [48,49].

To evaluate how the co-existing inorganic anions affect the photodegradation of STZ, the relevant experiments were well conducted at environmental concentrations of chloride (Cl−), sulfate (SO4 2−) and nitrate (NO3 −) [44]. Seen from Fig. 9(b-d), the Kapp value decreased to 0.0300 min−1 after adding Cl−, but gradually increased to 0.0771 min−1 when introduced SO4 2−. According to previous studies, Cl− was able to react with •OH to generate Cl• which possessed weak oxidation potential [50]. Hence the photocatalytic activity of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system decreased after addition Cl−. Conversely, when SO4 2− was present in the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system, the •OH would react with SO4 2− to form SO4 •−. Compared to •OH, SO4 •− had a longer lifetime and higher standard redox potential, which accelerated the STZ photolysis process [43]. In term of NO3 −, the degradation process of STZ was rapidly boosted by NO3 − in the low concentration range of 5 ∼ 20 mg/L. However, when the content of NO3 − increased to 30 mg/L, the promotion effect of NO3 − on STZ degradation was declined (Kapp = 0.0552 min−1). This was because NO3 − in natural water was usually equipped to form •OH under illumination without consuming any radical species and •OH had high reactivity to sulfonamides [51], which improved the apparent reaction rate constant (Kapp) of STZ removal. Moreover, the strong light absorption characteristic of NO3 − would transform into predominant at the high content of NO3 − (30 mg/L), Which could suppress the above beneficial effect [49]. Apparently, the minimum degradation efficiency of STZ was still 92.96% after adding multiple anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −), which revealed 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was an attractive material for wastewater remediation.

The charge carriers migration path was always concluded from the result of quenching experiments. Fig. 10 (a) showed the Kapp of 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst which were from photocatalytic kinetics after adding IPA (trap •OH), K2Cr2O7 (trap e−), BQ (trap •O2 –) and EDTA (trap h+) (Fig. S9(a, b)). It could be seen that the Kapp respectively dropped from 0.0348 min−1 to 0.0015, 0.0187, 0.0195 and 0.0345 min−1 in the presence of BQ, EDTA, K2Cr2O7 and IPA. The observation manifested that •O2 –, h+ and e− were important active species contributing to the removal of STZ by 50% BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst, yet •OH hardly worked in 50% BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system. In comparison with 50% BiOCOOH/uCN, the Kapp of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN nanocomposite after adding scavengers decreased to 0.001 min−1 (remove •O2 –), 0.0303 min−1 (remove e−), 0.0383 min−1 (remove •OH) and 0.0411 min−1 (remove h+). It was indicated that STZ degradation in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system was dependent on the active species of •O2 –, e−, •OH and h+, and •OH had enormous contribution. It was noteworthy that the kind of the dominant reactive species was different between 50% BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system, which would be deeply discussed in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydroxyl radical (•OH) and superoxide radical (•O2 –) determination
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of •OH and •O2 – in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system was further verified by TA and NBT molecular probe respectively. Specifically, •OH would react with TA to yield fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid. As depicted in Fig. 10(b), the PL signals of TA reaction solution at 425 nm increased with the irradiation time, revealing that •OH was endlessly produced on the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN surface during LED visible illumination. However, there was fewer •OH in 50% BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system than 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Vis system (Fig. S9(c)), which was consistent with electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of DMPO–•OH (Fig. 10(d)). Furthermore, •O2 – could react with NBT to form the insoluble formazan, decreasing the intensity of absorption peak at 259 nm. Thus the absorption peak intensity of NBT solution was inversely proportional to the amount of •O2 – in photodegradation reaction. As shown in Fig. S9(d), the absorption peak of reaction solution descended as illumination time went on. Besides, the DMPO–•O2 – adduct characteristic signal appeared in the ESR spectra of the 50% BiOCOOH/uCN and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN, which suggested that •O2 – radicals were the active species of two samples.

The band value was estimated by the Mulliken electronegativity theory, which was seen as the empirical equation (Eqs. (2–3)) [32]:
(2)
E VB
= X - -
E
e
+ 0.5
Eg
(3)
E CB
=
E VB
- - Eg
where EVB/ECB, X and Ee denote the positions of valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB), the electronegativity of the material (ca. 4.72 eV for uCN, 5.53 eV for BiOCOOH) and the reduction potential of water (4.5 eV vs. vacuum), respectively. Scheme 1 vividly portrayed the calculative EVB of uCN and BiOCOOH were 1.57 eV and 2.77 eV, and the ECB of them were – 1.13 eV and – 0.71 eV, respectively.

The quenching experiments and •OH radicals determined by fluorescence spectroscopy analysis revealed •OH radicals were actively yielded in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-LED system and which were few in 50% BiOCOOH/uCN-LED system. Hence, it could be deduced that the charge transfer of 50% BiOCOOH/uCN hybrid was the conventional Type-II mechanism, in keeping with previous research [22]. Nevertheless, if the photogenerated electrons of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN hybrid followed the conventional Type-II transfer mechanism, the accumulated electrons on the CB of uCN were not able to produce •OH (2.4 eV vs. NHE) [52]. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite might be efficiently transferred via the vectorial transfer pathway (CBBiOCOOH → CODs → VBuCN), decreasing the recombination rate of electron and hole pairs. In this pathway, the h+ would gather on the VB of BiOCOOH (2.77 eV) which was higher than the production potential of •OH. All in all, CQDs were used as solid state mediators in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN hybrid to shuttle electrons between BiOCOOH and uCN, which not only improved the separation rates of photogenerated electrons and holes, but also elevated the redox ability of photogenerated electrons and holes.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN composite was shown in Scheme 1. Clearly, under the LED visible light irradiation, the e− of uCN was excited to the CB and corresponding h+ was left behind in VB. Subsequently, the e− in the CB of BiOCOOH transferred to CQDs and then continued to the VB of uCN where they recombine with h+ present on the VB. As a consequence, the h+ in BiOCOOH with strong oxidation potential could turn into •OH. Meanwhile, the e− in uCN with high reduction ability was quickly captured by O2 to develop •O2 −, and then took part in contaminants degradation.

The MS/MS spectra of STZ photodegradation over 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN under LED lamp irradiation were exhibited in Fig. S10. Fig. S10 suggested that there were seventeen major intermediates and named P1-P17. As shown in Scheme 2 , P1 and P2 were generated by S-N bond cleavage through reactive species attack, and P1 would be respectively converted to P3 and P4 by hydroxylation and deamination. The S-C bond was split to form P5 and P6, and the hydroxylation of P5 would yield P7 and P8. The intermediate P7 was hydroxylated to form P9 and the subsequent dehydrogenation produced P10. P11 might derive from the rupture of C-N fragment, which was also identified in EF system [53]. The hydroxylation of benzene ring or thiazole ring leaded to the generation of P12, P13 and P14, afterwards P15 was developed by the thiazole ring break of P14. Additionally, the firsthand oxidation of amino group on the benzene ring in STZ gave rise to the appearance of P16. And the photoproduct P17 might be attributed to substitution reaction, which was in line with the report by Deng et al [54]. As has been noted, pivotal pathways of STZ degradation contained the bond break, hydroxylation, oxidation, as well as substitution reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recyclability and effect of water matrix on removal of STZ
ce:section.ce:para: The reusability and practicability of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst were tested by cycling experiments and various real water samples. After every cycle, the catalyst was recovered by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 5 min) and rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, respectively. Then, the catalyst was dried for 12 h at 60 °C. The reused experiments for 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN degradation of STZ were conducted in a similar condition to that of the fresh catalyst. As displayed in Fig. 11 (a), the removal efficiency of STZ was 99.28% ± 1.04, 96.08% ± 1.75, 97.38% ± 0.60, 97.04% ± 0.90, and 97.35% ± 1.57 for the first, second, third, fourth, and fifth run respectively, revealing the superior stability of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalyst. Furthermore, the XRD patterns, FTIR spectra and SEM images of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN before and after sulfathiazole removal had no visible changes (Fig. S11). The used 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was further characterized by XPS and the result was shown in Fig. S12. It was clear to see that no obvious changes were observed in the peaks of the used 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN sample, which corresponded to the results of cycling tests. Moreover, Fig. 11(b) illustrated the effect of real natural water and wastewater (namely tap water (TW), river water (RW) and wastewater treatment plant effluent (WTPE)) on the degradation of STZ using 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN. It was clear that the removal efficiencies in the actual water samples were inferior compared to STZ-polluted deionized water (DW) after a 90 min reaction, along with the Kapp of 0.0289 min−1 (TW), 0.0216 min−1 (RW) and 0.0211 min−1 (WTPE). The photocatalytic activities of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN in real water matrices were dropped, which might due to the exceedingly intricate components of actual water samples. Nonetheless, with extended illumination time, the removal efficiencies of STZ by 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN in the natural water or wastewater all reach above 96%, suggesting that the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN could be used in STZ-contaminated water purification. All in all, the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN nanocomposite not only indicated supported reusability in recycling runs, but also remained satisfactory photocatalytic activities of STZ in actual water matrices.

To confirm the practicability of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-LED lamp system, a 300 W Xenon lamp (HSX-F300, simulated sunlight) was also utilized as light source. It was observed from Fig. S13 that the Kapp of STZ degradation in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Xenon lamp system was 0.1301 min−1, which was much higher than that in 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-LED lamp system. This increase was probably caused by the up-converted photoluminescence property of CQDs [18] and the broader spectrum of Xenon lamp (200 nm – 1100 nm). In previous work, EEO was defined as the electrical energy for removing an order of magnitude of pollutants in 1 m3 of contaminated solution and was determined by the Eqs. (4) [55].
(4)
E EO
=
38.4 × P
/
V × k
where P is the sum of the input power (kw), V is the reaction solution volume (L), k is the pseudo first order rate contant (Kapp, min−1). Therefore, the EEO of other g-C3N4-based photocatalysts in reported researches and 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN in this study were calculated and summarized in Table 2 . It was evident that the EEO of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-LED lamp or 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Xenon lamp was inferior to other g-C3N4-based materials, indicating that 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN complex had excellent photocatalytic activity and low energy consumption. Obviously, the energy-saving effectiveness of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-LED lamp (EEO = 364) was more conspicuous than 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Xenon lamp (EEO = 885). Besides, LED lamp has long service life, excellent mechanical stability, very little heat generation and without hazardous mercury [58–60]. Therefore, LED lamp as visible light source was more feasible for the practical application.

The novel CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN photocatalysts were successfully constructed by thermal polymerization and subsequent hydrothermal process, where CQDs played the role of electron shuttle bridges as expected. SEM, TEM, EDS, XRD and FTIR analysis illustrated that the flower-like structure of BiOCOOH and the layer structure of g-C3N4, and XPS analysis showed the coexistence of Bi, O, C, N in 4- CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN. After 90 min of LED lamp irradiation, the 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN (99.28%) showed higher degradation efficiency of STZ than pure BiOCOOH, uCN and 50% BiOCOOH/uCN. Compared with 50% BiOCOOH/uCN, the introduction of CQDs enhanced the photocatalytic activity, which was likely because of the fast photogenerated charges transfer and separation on the surface of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN. Furthermore, the dominant reactive species to induce the degradation of STZ were •O2 – and •OH. The STZ degradation pathways were deduced that mainly included the bond break, hydroxylation, oxidation, as well as substitution reaction based on HRMS analysis. Meanwhile, 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN possessed good reusability, and its removal efficiency of STZ was about 99.28% ± 1.04, 96.08% ± 1.75, 97.38% ± 0.60, 97.04% ± 0.90, and 97.35% ± 1.57 for the first, second, third, fourth, and fifth run respectively. Satisfactorily, 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN maintained superior STZ removal rate in deionized water (99.28% ± 1.04), top water (97.15% ± 1.16), river water (96.44% ± 1.20) and wastewater treatment plant effluent (96.19% ± 0.76). More importantly, the evaluation of energy consumption for STZ photodegradation by CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN manifested that the energy-saving effectiveness of 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-LED lamp (EEO = 364) or 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN-Xenon lamp (EEO = 885) was more conspicuous than other g-C3N4-based materials. Overall, 4-CQDs/BiOCOOH/uCN was a promising photocatalyst owing to its remarkable performance, high-stability, energy-efficient and low cost.
